flashcards.front: What is the fundamental principle of a stack data structure?
flashcards.back: Last In, First Out (LIFO) - the last element inserted is the first one to be removed.

flashcards.front: Name the five basic operations performed on a stack.
flashcards.back: push() - add element to top, pop() - remove element from top, peek()/top() - view top element, isEmpty() - check if empty, isFull() - check if full (array implementation).
A runtime error that occurs when trying to push an element into a stack that is already full (in array-based implementation with fixed size).
A runtime error that occurs when trying to pop or peek an element from an empty stack.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of push and pop operations in a stack?
flashcards.back: O(1) - constant time complexity for both push and pop operations.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of an array-based stack implementation?
flashcards.back: O(n) where n is the maximum capacity of the stack.

flashcards.front: How is a stack implemented using an array?
flashcards.back: Using a fixed-size array with a top pointer/index that tracks the position of the last inserted element; increments on push, decrements on pop.

flashcards.front: How is a stack implemented using a linked list?
flashcards.back: By treating the head of the linked list as the top of the stack; push adds new node at head, pop removes from head - O(1) operations.

flashcards.front: What are the two main advantages of linked list implementation over array implementation of stacks?
flashcards.back: 1. Dynamic size (no overflow unless system memory exhausts) 2. No memory wastage (allocates as needed).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between peek() and pop() operations?
flashcards.back: peek() returns the top element without removing it; pop() returns and removes the top element.

flashcards.front: What is an application of stack in function call management?
flashcards.back: Function call stack (recursion) - stores return addresses, local variables, and parameters when a function is called, and restores them when function returns.

flashcards.front: What is Polish Notation (PN) and how does a stack help in its evaluation?
flashcards.back: Operators precede their operands (e.g., +AB). Stack is used to push operands and pop them when an operator is encountered to perform the operation.

flashcards.front: What is Reverse Polish Notation (RPN) and its advantage?
flashcards.back: Operators come after operands (e.g., AB+). Eliminates need for parentheses and precedence rules; evaluated easily using a stack from left to right.

flashcards.front: How is a stack used in expression conversion (infix to postfix)?
flashcards.back: Stack holds operators and parentheses during conversion; higher precedence operators are popped before pushing lower precedence ones; parentheses are handled using special rules.

flashcards.front: What is the initial value of the top pointer in an empty stack?
flashcards.back: In array implementation, top = -1 (indicates empty stack). In linked list, top = NULL (null pointer).
